plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open direct mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, or MFU (all support instant KYC and low-cost direct fund investing). Step 2: Create three named goal buckets within one account: \"Apartment Fund,\" \"5Cr Goal,\" and \"FI Wealth\" for centralized tracking. Step 3: Enable auto-debit monthly SIP for each fund on day 1 or day 15 (sync with salary date). Step 4: Select \"Growth\" option for all funds to reinvest dividends; avoid \"Dividend\" options. Step 5: Invest ₹20L lumpsum today split proportionally across all 11 funds (₹10L into Portfolio B and ₹10L into Portfolio C). Monthly SIPs commence from next month on auto-debit."
review_checkpoints: Every 6 months: Verify all SIPs are executing correctly. Check no auto-debits failed due to bank issues. Restart any paused SIPs immediately. Do NOT obsess over NAV daily — this is normal volatility.

Annually (on birthday): Review each portfolio's 1-year return vs. category benchmarks. If any fund underperforms by >3% for 2 consecutive years, consider swapping to a top performer in the same category. Do NOT overreact to single bad year — judge on 3–5 year performance.
After 3 years: Portfolio A maturity. Liquidate apartment fund (~₹230–240L expected) for down payment. Redirect ₹15L/month to Portfolios B & C.
After 10 years: Review ₹5Cr milestone in Portfolio B. If target hit or exceeded, either de-risk to conservative or consolidate into Portfolio C. Reassess continued monthly SIP levels.

After 15 years: Mid-course correction for Portfolio C (20-year plan). Review if allocations still match risk profile. Begin gradual shift toward debt (10–15%) and gold (5%) if approaching semi-retirement mindset.

After 20 years: Portfolio C matures (age 53). Assess financial independence status. Review withdrawal strategy, rebalance to 80% equity / 15% debt / 5% gold, plan for sustainable income generation phase.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Rebalance annually (ideally January) only if any asset category drifts >10 percentage points from target. For Portfolio A (3-year horizon), avoid rebalancing near maturity (year 2.5+) to lock in gains and minimize transaction costs. For Portfolio B & C, rebalance by selling outperforming categories and buying underperformers to reset allocations. Example: If midcap grows from 30% target to 42% (due to outperformance), sell 6% midcap and buy 3% small-cap + 3% large-cap. Never rebalance during market crashes (hold and keep SIPing — downturns are buying opportunities). If markets fall 25–30%, maintain allocations and continue monthly SIPs to average down cost basis."
plan.rationale: This three-portfolio structure aligns with Jay's multi-goal investment thesis. Portfolio A (₹15L/month × 3 years) focuses on large-cap and large-midcap equity for capital preservation with growth in the apartment fund, targeting 23% CAGR base case (~₹230–240L by year 3). Portfolio B (₹2.5L/month × 10 years) is aggressive growth across all cap sizes (20% large-cap, 30% large-midcap, 30% midcap, 20% small-cap), targeting 20.8% CAGR (~₹500–550L post-tax by year 10). Portfolio C (₹1.35L/month × 20 years) combines passive midcap indexing (30% for low-cost core) with active managers in small/mid/multicap segments, targeting 18.5% CAGR (~₹2.2–2.5Cr by age 53). All funds are equity-taxed (direct mutual fund growth option, 12.5% LTCG tax on gains >₹1.25L annually) rather than debt-taxed, as equity returns dramatically outpace debt after tax optimization for the 30% slab. Total monthly commitment: ₹18.85L, leaving ₹1.15L buffer for taxes, lifestyle, and market opportunities.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Inception — Full equity allocation across all three portfolios. Portfolio A (apartment fund) weighted toward large-cap (40%) + large-midcap (60%). Portfolio B (₹5Cr goal) diversified 20/30/30/20 (large/L&M/mid/small). Portfolio C (FI wealth) 30% passive index + 70% active (small/mid/multicap).
Apartment fund maturity. Liquidate Portfolio A (target ₹230–240L) and deploy for down payment. Redirect ₹15L/month SIP capacity to boost Portfolio B or C based on market conditions and opportunity.

₹5Cr milestone review. If target reached (likely >₹500L post-tax), optionally shift Portfolio B to conservative (5–10% debt/fixed income) or consolidate into Portfolio C and extend wealth-building. Alternative: maintain 100% equity if markets remain favorable and risk tolerance intact.

Financial independence phase (age 53). Portfolio C matures to ₹2.2–2.5Cr. Begin gradual de-risking: shift 20% equity → debt/fixed income/gold to stabilize portfolio and reduce volatility. Reassess withdrawal strategy.

aps.reasoning: This is a fundamentally ACTIVE investment plan with passive characteristics in execution philosophy. PASSIVE_INSTRUMENT_FRACTION (0.02): Only the Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index fund (0.2% ER) at 30% allocation within Portfolio C represents passive equity exposure. Aggregating across all three portfolios and their monthly SIP weights (A: 1.5M, B: 0.25M, C: 0.135M total), passive exposure is ~2% of total equity. The remaining 98% is actively managed (Large Cap, L&Midcap, Midcap, Small Cap, Multicap categories). TURNOVER_SCORE (0.85): The plan exhibits strong buy-and-hold discipline with annual rebalancing only if any category drifts >10 percentage points—a very high threshold. Review checkpoints occur at major milestones (3, 10, 15, 20 years), not quarterly. The plan explicitly states 'Never rebalance during market crashes (hold and keep SIPing—downturns are buying opportunities).' Fund swaps only occur if underperformance persists >2 years. This is consistent with low-turnover, long-term indexing philosophy. COST_EMPHASIS_SCORE (0.25): Cost is mentioned (e.g., 'Low-cost (0.2%) midcap index diversification'), but fund selection is primarily driven by alpha values (4.27% to 15.17%), Sharpe ratios (1.00), and 3–5 year historical returns. The plan emphasizes 'strong alpha,' 'proven performer,' and 'star performer' as selection criteria, not 'minimize expense ratio.' Tax optimization (LTCG vs. dividend) is discussed, showing some cost consciousness, but expense ratios are secondary. RESEARCH_VS_COST_SCORE (0.55): The plan performs quantitative fund analysis (alpha, Sharpe ratio, return history, AUM stability) but no deep company-level security research (no PEG ratios, competitive moats, management quality analysis, or sector rotation thesis). It is fund-picking, not stock-picking. The inclusion of a passive index component (Motilal Midcap 150) with explicit rationale ('low-cost core') suggests acknowledgment that broad market exposure can be appropriate. The alpha-focused selection of actively managed funds demonstrates some conviction, but not fundamental research depth. RESEARCH_VS_COST_SCORE reflects this middle ground. TIME_HORIZON_ALIGNMENT_SCORE (0.90): The plan is explicitly designed around three distinct time horizons (3, 10, 20 years for the three portfolios). Monthly SIPs with auto-debit enforce disciplined, long-term capital deployment. The rationale states 'downturns are buying opportunities' and the plan recommends maintaining allocations and 'continue monthly SIPs to average down cost basis' during crashes—classic long-term, patient investor behavior. Rebalancing thresholds (10% drift tolerance) and infrequent review cycles reinforce decades-long compounding. PORTFOLIO_ACTIVENESS_SCORE (0.15): The equity portfolio is genuinely active with strong, documented alpha across 98% of holdings. HDFC Midcap (30% of Portfolio B weight) shows alpha 14.48%; Edelweiss Midcap (25% of Portfolio C) shows alpha 15.17%; ICICI L&M (30% of Portfolio B) shows alpha 8.55%. These are high-conviction, high-tracking-error, alpha-positive bets. Only the Motilal Nifty Midcap 150 Index (2% of total equity) is passive. The plan explicitly states 'Portfolio C combines passive midcap indexing (30% for low-cost core) with **active managers in small/mid/multicap segments**.' This is a portfolio of true active managers with measurable alpha, not closet indexers. The low passive_instrument_fraction (0.02) combined with substantial documented alpha across all major holdings confirms genuine active management conviction. OVERALL PROFILE: APS ~0.30 (moderately active). The plan exhibits passive discipline in execution (low turnover, long time horizon, buy-and-hold mindset) but actively sources managers with high alpha and minimal index exposure. This is an 'active selection of active managers for patient, long-term wealth building'—not a passive index portfolio, but not a frequent-trading, tactical portfolio either.

pqs.reasoning: CRITICAL FLAW: The plan is financially unachievable. Investor has ₹3L monthly investible surplus but plan commits ₹18.85L/month in ongoing SIPs across all three portfolios simultaneously (₹15L Portfolio A + ₹2.5L Portfolio B + ₹1.35L Portfolio C = ₹18.85L). This is 6.28x the available capacity. The plan's closing statement "₹18.85L, leaving ₹1.15L buffer for taxes, lifestyle, and market opportunities" implies a ₹20L total monthly budget that doesn't exist. Even if the initial ₹20L lumpsum comes from existing ₹2.5Cr corpus, the recurring SIP structure is impossible. LUMPSUM INCONSISTENCY: Setup phase states "Invest ₹20L lumpsum today split proportionally across all 11 funds (₹10L Portfolio B, ₹10L Portfolio C)" but allocations show ₹30M lumpsum deployed (₹8M + ₹12M + ₹2M + ₹3M + ₹3M + ₹2M into first 6 funds), with no clear mapping to stated ₹20L. GOAL ALIGNMENT: The three-portfolio structure (3-year apartment, 10-year ₹5Cr, 20-year FI) is conceptually sound for the investor's goals, but unachievable at stated monthly commitment levels. Given ₹3L/month capacity, a realistic plan would allocate roughly ₹1.5L/month across three portfolios, not ₹18.85L/month. DIVERSIFICATION: Strong—10 funds across 8 fund houses with excellent coverage of large/midcap/small-cap. Fund selection (Motilal, HDFC, ICICI, Edelweiss, Bandhan, etc.) shows quality awareness. 100% equity is appropriate for 20-year horizon but leaves zero diversification to debt/gold. RISK-RETURN: Risk profile matches investor's aggressive appetite and comfort with volatility. However, projections (23% Portfolio A, 20.8% Portfolio B, 18.5% Portfolio C) assume full deployment of stated SIPs—unachievable projections are misleading. TAX EFFICIENCY: MAJOR OMISSION—No ELSS despite being in 30% slab. Investor could claim ₹1.5L annual 80C deduction worth ₹45k/year tax savings (₹450k+ over 10 years) while still receiving equity tax treatment (12.5% LTCG). This is a critical miss for old-regime planning. Plan correctly identifies equity-tax advantage (12.5% LTCG vs 30% slab) and specifies direct funds + growth options, but fails to maximize 80C. No discussion of LTCG threshold (₹1.25L annually) or tax impact of rebalancing. INTERNAL CONSISTENCY: Fund-to-portfolio mapping is opaque—allocation percentages (40%, 60%, 20%, 30%, 30%, 20%, 30%, 25%, 25%, 20%) don't clearly map to three goal-buckets. Rebalancing guidelines and review checkpoints are logically sound but cannot redeem the core capacity flaw. RECOMMENDATION: Plan needs complete restructuring. Realistic monthly allocation for ₹3L capacity might be: ₹1.2L Portfolio A (apartment), ₹1.0L Portfolio B (₹5Cr), ₹0.8L Portfolio C (FI). Add ELSS ₹1.5L annually. Acknowledge that ₹5Cr 10-year target may require portfolio B to extend 12–15 years at achievable capacity, or rely on lumpsum deployments from portfolio A liquidations and corpus opportunism.
